citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
A young adult male zebra (1 ½ years old) had a veterinary care issue that the licensee noticed but did not notify the
attending veterinarian. Per facility representatives, last fall (approximately September 2025), the young male zebra was
injured by fighting with the 6-year-old dominant zebra stallion, who was housed within the same outdoor primary
enclosure with other zebras, oryxes, elands, and blackbucks. The injured zebra had a bloody wound at his left back hip
area, as described to the APHIS official. The facility representatives stated they didn’t communicate this wound to the
attending veterinarian, and the APHIS official confirmed the veterinarian didn't know of the injury upon interview. After the
injury, this zebra isolated himself against the rear of the enclosure, away from the rest of the animals, per facility
representatives, and the wound eventually healed after one month. Failure to promptly identify such conditions or to
communicate abnormal findings with the veterinarian in a timely manner delays appropriate treatment and causes undue
pain/distress to the animals. Daily observation of all animals is necessary to assess their health and well-being and may
be accomplished by someone other than the attending veterinarian. However, a mechanism of direct and frequent
communication is required so timely and accurate information on problems of animal health, behavior and well-being is
conveyed to the attending veterinarian to allow for appropriate diagnosis and treatment.
Correct from this day forward, 02/13/2026.

citations.desc: Procurement of dogs, cats, and other animals; dealers.
citations.narrative:
The licensee purchased two oryxes, four blackbucks, and two elands on February 20, 2025, from a person that did not
have an active USDA license. As a USDA-licensed exhibitor, the licensee is required to obtain animals from licensed
persons, if those persons are required to have a license. The person supplying these animals to this licensee is required
to be licensed, as they were supplying exotic hoofstock, and do not qualify for exemptions from the licensing
requirements. Failure to ensure that sources of animals, which are required to be licensed, do indeed have a valid license
prevents APHIS from ensuring the well-being of those animals at their originating facility, and that all parties are in
compliance with the AWA. The licensee shall ensure that animals are only obtained from sources that hold a current,
valid, and unsuspended license or from individuals that are exempted from licensure under the AWA.
Correct from this date forward, February 13, 2026.

The facility has an incomplete contingency plan. The only identified emergencies are weather-related emergencies
without sufficient details provided. Also, it does not address how to recover an animal if one escapes or the details of the
evacuation plan in case of fire. A complete written contingency plan is required to help ensure animal health and well-
being in the event of an emergency or disaster; without this tool, animals may suffer illness, injury, or death due to
unpreparedness. Correct by developing, documenting and ensuring the written contingency plan includes the
identification of situations that might be experienced to include animal escapes, fire, electrical outages, and natural
disasters common to the area such as tornadoes and ice/snowstorms. The plan should also outline the specific tasks
required to be carried out in response to the identified emergencies to include such topics as sources of backup food and
water as well as sanitation, ventilation, bedding, veterinary care, and heating/cooling. Finally, a chain of command needs
to be identified while also addressing how response and recovery will be handled in terms of materials, resources, and
training needed.
Correct by: 04/01/2026.

There is no documentation maintained to indicate that an annual review of the contingency plan has been completed. The
contingency plan must be reviewed by the facility on at least an annual basis to ensure that it adequately addresses all
required contingency-related criteria and that no updates or changes need to be made to the plan such as changes made
as a result of any recent unforeseen circumstance or the addition of new species. The facility must maintain
documentation of their contingency plan annual reviews including documenting any amendments or changes made since
the previous year's review.
Correct by: 04/01/2026.

A 1 ½ year old male zebra, who was injured by a 6-year-old zebra stallion previously (Fall 2025), was killed by the same
stallion on December 10, 2025, per facility representatives. After the initial injury, these two zebras were not separated
and they continued to be housed in the same outdoor primary enclosure, despite some continued fighting. The
incompatibility of the zebras within their shared outdoor enclosure resulted in animal injury, stress, and ultimately the
younger zebra’s death. Animals housed in the same primary enclosure must be compatible. Animals should not be
housed with others that interfere with their health or cause them discomfort.
Correct by: 03/01/2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3)  Critical
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
A young adult male zebra (1 ½ years old) had a veterinary care issue that the licensee noticed but did not notify the

communicate abnormal findings with the veterinarian in a timely manner delays appropriate treatment and causes undue

communication is required so timely and accurate information on problems of animal health, behavior and well-being is 
conveyed to the attending veterinarian to allow for appropriate diagnosis and treatment. 
Correct from this day forward, 02/13/2026. 
2.132(d) 
   
Procurement of dogs, cats, and other animals; dealers. 
The licensee purchased two oryxes, four blackbucks, and two elands on February 20, 2025, from a person that did not 
have an active USDA license. As a USDA-licensed exhibitor, the licensee is required to obtain animals from licensed 
persons, if those persons are required to have a license. The person supplying these animals to this licensee is required 
to be licensed, as they were supplying exotic hoofstock, and do not qualify for exemptions from the licensing 
requirements. Failure to ensure that sources of animals, which are required to be licensed, do indeed have a valid license 
prevents APHIS from ensuring the well-being of those animals at their originating facility, and that all parties are in 
compliance with the AWA. The licensee shall ensure that animals are only obtained from sources that hold a current, 
valid, and unsuspended license or from individuals that are exempted from licensure under the AWA. 
Correct from this date forward, February 13, 2026. 
2.134(a)

Contingency planning. 
The facility has an incomplete contingency plan. The only identified emergencies are weather-related emergencies 
without sufficient details provided. Also, it does not address how to recover an animal if one escapes or the details of the 
evacuation plan in case of fire. A complete written contingency plan is required to help ensure animal health and well-
being in the event of an emergency or disaster; without this tool, animals may suffer illness, injury, or death due to 
unpreparedness. Correct by developing, documenting and ensuring the written contingency plan includes the 
identification of situations that might be experienced to include animal escapes, fire, electrical outages, and natural 
disasters common to the area such as tornadoes and ice/snowstorms. The plan should also outline the specific tasks 
required to be carried out in response to the identified emergencies to include such topics as sources of backup food and 
water as well as sanitation, ventilation, bedding, veterinary care, and heating/cooling. Finally, a chain of command needs 
to be identified while also addressing how response and recovery will be handled in terms of materials, resources, and 
training needed. 
Correct by: 04/01/2026. 
2.134(b) 
   
Contingency planning. 
There is no documentation maintained to indicate that an annual review of the contingency plan has been completed. The 
contingency plan must be reviewed by the facility on at least an annual basis to ensure that it adequately addresses all 
required contingency-related criteria and that no updates or changes need to be made to the plan such as changes made 
as a result of any recent unforeseen circumstance or the addition of new species. The facility must maintain 
documentation of their contingency plan annual reviews including documenting any amendments or changes made since 
the previous year's review. 
Correct by: 04/01/2026. 
3.133  Critical
   
Separation. 
A 1 ½ year old male zebra, who was injured by a 6-year-old zebra stallion previously (Fall 2025), was killed by the same 
stallion on December 10, 2025, per facility representatives. After the initial injury, these two zebras were not separated 
and they continued to be housed in the same outdoor primary enclosure, despite some continued fighting. The 
incompatibility of the zebras within their shared outdoor enclosure resulted in animal injury, stress, and ultimately the 
younger zebra’s death. Animals housed in the same primary enclosure must be compatible. Animals should not be 
housed with others that interfere with their health or cause them discomfort.  
Correct by: 03/01/2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
